source.title: William David Ross
source.venue: Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy (Metaphysics Research Lab, Stanford University)

confidence_rationale: SEP is canonical (high source quality). Ross's framework is the dominant modern alternative to Kantian absolutism, and it directly addresses the form of Q-026 by arguing that no action is context-independently required: prima facie duties are universal but actual duty is always settled comparatively in context. This is a substantive challenge to the very form of Q-026, so the relevance to the question is high even though the mapping to REE's specific axioms is indirect.
failure_signatures: Ross argues that determining one's actual duty in any concrete situation 'rests with perception' and yields only 'probable opinion, never certainty' -- which directly undermines any reading of Q-026 that expects a list of strictly required actions independent of context.

On Ross's view, an act's actual rightness 'belongs to it in virtue of its whole nature and of nothing less than this' -- meaning the complete contextual situation is non-eliminably part of what makes an action obligatory. Q-026's 'independent of context' clause is, on this reading, asking the wrong question.

mapping.source_claim_statement: There is a set of universal prima facie duties (fidelity, reparation, gratitude, beneficence, non-maleficence) that hold in all contexts, but no action is absolutely required in every context: actual duty is always determined by comparing the relative weight of competing prima facie duties in the specific situation.
mapping.ree_translation: Q-026 presupposes that some actions are strictly required at all times. Ross's framework offers a serious philosophical challenge to that presupposition: the universal/context-independent thing is the *consideration set* (the prima facie duties), not the *action*. For REE this would suggest reframing Q-026 -- perhaps the axioms (INV-025..029) generate a universal *consideration set* analogous to Ross's prima facie duties, but the actual required action in any moment is settled by salience-weighted comparison in context (which is closer to how REE's harm/goal/residue machinery already works).
mapping.mapping_caveat: Ross's prima facie duties are not derived from REE's axiom set, and his framework is not committed to anything like INV-027 (world-existence) or INV-029 (love). The connection is structural: Ross provides a model for how universal moral content can coexist with context-sensitive action selection. That structural lesson may apply to REE's axiom system, but the specific duty list does not transfer.
mapping.source_context: Philosophy of ethics; canonical secondary literature on Ross's pluralism. Not empirical.

confidence_components.notes: High source quality (SEP, peer-edited). Mapping fidelity moderate -- the structural lesson (universal considerations, contextual action selection) maps cleanly onto REE's existing architecture, but Ross's specific duty list does not. Transfer risk moderate because the framework is normative-philosophical and applying it to a computational agent is a real interpretive step.
